definition: Encapsulate a request as an object so actions can be queued, logged, retried, or undone independently of the invoker.
ruleOfThumb: When actions need history, retries, or scheduling, model them as command objects instead of inline method calls.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: What limitation appears when product asks for undo and retry for these actions?

options.text: Actions are hard-coded direct calls, so there is no first-class action object to record, queue, retry, or undo consistently
options.explanation: Without command objects, behavior is tied to click handlers. Features like history, delayed execution, and generic retry become ad-hoc.

options.text: Toolbar should be a singleton so command history is globally accessible
options.explanation: Singleton scope does not solve action encapsulation or provide reusable execution metadata.

options.text: The issue is that editor methods should return booleans indicating success
options.explanation: Result values may help, but the structural gap is missing command abstraction.

options.text: These handlers should be static methods for performance
options.explanation: Static dispatch does not provide action history or decoupled invocation.

questions.prompt: A job runner must execute user actions later and retry failures. What design best fits?

options.text: Represent each action as a command object with execute() so the runner can queue and retry actions uniformly
options.explanation: Command objects decouple the invoker from concrete action logic and make persistence/replay straightforward.

options.text: Keep the if statements and add more action types; retries can be done with recursion
options.explanation: Inline branching scales poorly and mixes invocation, dispatch, and retry concerns in one place.

options.text: Use Template Method because archive and restore share one line each
options.explanation: Template Method addresses algorithm skeletons, not request encapsulation for deferred execution.

options.text: Use Observer so actions broadcast themselves to repositories
options.explanation: Observer addresses subscription fan-out, not representing executable requests with replay semantics.

questions.prompt: Which version is harder to extend when the product adds action history and undo?

options.text: Version A — requests are strings plus branching logic, so undo/history requires custom handling for every action path
options.explanation: A central conditional invoker becomes complex as actions grow. B treats each request as an object with consistent execute/undo behavior.

options.text: Version B — command objects over-abstract simple actions and make history impossible to persist
options.explanation: Command objects are exactly what make history persistence and replay practical.
